title: Fractions to Decimals for Kids | 5th Grade Math Explained
description:
Can't figure out how to turn fractions into decimals? This fun and easy math lesson breaks it all down — step by step — so any 5th grader can understand it!

In this video, you'll follow along with Gina as she tries to add up fractions with different denominators to order paint for her school play. Her friend Kate teaches her a super useful trick: convert fractions to decimals by turning the denominator into a power of ten (10, 100, or 1,000). Once you do that, adding and comparing becomes so much easier!

Here's what students will learn:
✅ How to convert fractions to decimals step by step
✅ Why decimals are based on powers of ten
✅ The difference between terminating decimals and non-terminating decimals
✅ How to use a calculator to divide the numerator by the denominator
✅ Real-world examples that make fractions and decimals make sense

This video is perfect for 5th grade students learning about fractions and decimals, parents helping with math homework, and teachers looking for an engaging classroom resource. The friendly storytelling style — including a hilarious cartoon segment set in Fractionville and Decimal Town — makes this one of the most memorable ways to learn how to change fractions to decimals.

Want to ace your next math test? Watch the full video, take notes, and practice the examples along the way!

👍 Like this video and subscribe for more elementary math lessons that actually make sense!
timestamps:
00:00 Introduction – Gina's Fraction Problem
01:45 Kate Explains How to Convert Fractions to Decimals
03:30 Making Denominators into Powers of Ten
05:10 Fractionville and Decimal Town – Animated Story
08:00 Terminating vs. Non-Terminating Decimals
10:20 History of Decimals with George at the Ontario Science Center
13:05 Using a Calculator to Divide Numerator by Denominator
thumbnail_brief: The thumbnail should feature a bright, cheerful classroom or cartoon-style background with bold yellow and blue colors to attract young viewers. A friendly cartoon character or illustrated child should be shown holding a large fraction (like 3/4) on one side with an arrow pointing to its decimal equivalent (0.75) on the other side, rendered in a large, clear font. The text overlay should read 'Fractions → Decimals MADE EASY!' in bold white letters with a dark drop shadow to ensure strong readability at small sizes.
